policycategories.PolicySubCategory: Occupational Health and Safety Risk Control

subpolicies.Description: To implement this subpolicy, the organization must establish a process for assessing and selecting contractors based on agreed specifications, terms, and conditions. The responsible department should conduct thorough assessments to ensure compliance with legal requirements and occupational health and safety standards. Documentation of assessments and selection criteria must be maintained for audit purposes.
subpolicies.Control: The Health and Safety Department is responsible for conducting contractor assessments and selecting contractors based on agreed specifications, terms, and conditions. Assessments must be conducted prior to engaging contractors and periodically thereafter. Assessment records, including compliance documentation and performance evaluations, must be stored securely for a minimum of [X] years. The success of this control is measured by the percentage of contractors meeting occupational health and safety requirements, monitored quarterly and reported to the Health and Safety Committee.

subpolicies.Description: Top management must demonstrate visible leadership and commitment to the OH&S management system. They are responsible for setting the direction, establishing OH&S objectives, and ensuring the availability of necessary resources. Top management should actively participate in OH&S activities, communicate the importance of safety, and promote a positive safety culture throughout the organization.
subpolicies.Control: Top management must conduct regular OH&S management system reviews at least annually to assess its effectiveness. The reviews should include evaluating the performance of the system, identifying opportunities for improvement, and ensuring compliance with ISO 45001 requirements. The reviews must be documented, and any identified issues or corrective actions must be addressed within specified timelines. The reviews should involve key stakeholders, including OH&S representatives and relevant management personnel.

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Establishment of Health and Safety Committees
subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy outlines the requirements for establishing and functioning health and safety committees within the organization. It includes the roles and responsibilities of committee members, the process for consultation and participation of workers, and the mechanisms for reporting incidents, hazards, risks, and opportunities.
subpolicies.Control: The Health and Safety Committees shall be established by top management, comprising representatives from various organizational levels. The committees shall meet regularly to discuss OH&S issues, review incident reports, identify hazards, and propose corrective actions. Workers shall have the right to report incidents, hazards, and risks without fear of reprisal. The committees shall maintain records of meetings, actions taken, and recommendations for continual improvement.

subpolicies.Description: Establish, implement, and maintain a process for consultation and participation of workers at all levels and functions in the OH&S management system. Provide mechanisms, time, training, and resources for consultation and participation. Ensure timely access to relevant information, remove obstacles to participation, and emphasize consultation and participation of non-managerial workers in various aspects of the OH&S management system.
subpolicies.Control: The organization shall establish, implement, and maintain a process for consultation and participation of workers, providing necessary mechanisms, time, training, and resources. Clear and relevant information about the OH&S management system must be provided timely. Obstacles to participation should be identified and removed. Non-managerial workers must be consulted on determining needs, establishing the OH&S policy, assigning roles, fulfilling legal requirements, establishing objectives, planning controls, monitoring, establishing audit programs, ensuring continual improvement, identifying hazards, assessing risks, determining competence requirements, communicating, implementing control measures, and investigating incidents and nonconformities.

subpolicies.Description: To implement the OH&S risk assessment process, the organization must define a methodology for assessing OH&S risks, including hazards and opportunities. This methodology should be proactive, systematic, and aligned with the organization's OH&S management system. Roles responsible for risk assessment, timelines for assessments, and criteria for evaluating risks must be clearly defined and documented.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S risk assessment methodology shall be implemented by the OH&S team, led by the OH&S manager, on a bi-annual basis. The assessment process will involve hazard identification, risk evaluation, and control effectiveness review using standardized tools and templates. The documented information on risk assessment methodologies and criteria shall be stored in a secure central repository for a minimum of 5 years. Success metrics include a 10% reduction in high-risk hazards identified per assessment cycle. Any exceptions or deviations from the assessment process must be approved by the OH&S manager and documented for review.

subpolicies.Description: To control documented information, the organization shall identify, describe, format, review, and approve all relevant information. It shall ensure availability, protection, and suitability of documented information through proper distribution, access control, storage, retention, and version control.
subpolicies.Control: The organization's OH&S management system shall identify, describe, format, review, and approve documented information. It shall control distribution, access, storage, retention, and changes to ensure availability, protection, and integrity of documented information. Responsible roles include OH&S managers, document controllers, and relevant process owners. Documented information shall be reviewed and updated annually, stored electronically in a secure repository, and retained for a minimum of 5 years. Changes to documented information shall be approved by the OH&S manager and documented with version control.

subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy provides detailed guidance on conducting monitoring and measurement activities to assess the organization's compliance with legal requirements and other obligations. It outlines the roles, processes, tools, and documentation required for effective monitoring and measurement.
subpolicies.Control: The Health and Safety Manager is responsible for overseeing the monitoring and measurement activities on a quarterly basis. The monitoring shall include regular inspections, audits, and data analysis to assess compliance levels. The results of the monitoring and measurement activities shall be documented in the Occupational Health and Safety Management System records. Any deviations from the set performance criteria shall trigger immediate corrective actions to address non-conformities.

subpolicies.Description: To implement the OH&S management system, the organization must designate responsibilities, define processes, and establish communication channels. Regular training and awareness programs should be conducted to ensure compliance with OH&S requirements.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S management system implementation must be overseen by the OH&S manager, with support from the HR department. Training sessions should be conducted quarterly for all employees, and records of attendance and topics covered must be maintained. Non-compliance with training requirements should be addressed through corrective actions.

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Interpretation and Application of Normative References
subpolicies.Description: To implement this subpolicy, organizations must ensure that all users understand and apply the normative references and terms as specified in ISO 45001. This includes using the correct verbal forms ('shall,' 'should,' 'may,' 'can') and following the guidance notes for clarity. Regular training and communication on the interpretation of terms are essential.
subpolicies.Control: All employees responsible for OH&S management must undergo training on the interpretation and application of normative references. Training sessions should be conducted annually by the OH&S manager or designated personnel. Records of training attendance and comprehension assessments must be maintained in the OH&S management system for a minimum of 3 years. Compliance will be monitored through periodic audits, with non-compliance leading to retraining and corrective actions.

subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy outlines the step-by-step process for reviewing and updating terms and definitions within the ISO 45001 standard. It includes roles and responsibilities, timelines for review, and criteria for modification.
subpolicies.Control: The Health and Safety Manager is responsible for conducting bi-annual reviews of all terms and definitions in the ISO 45001 documentation. Reviews shall be completed within 30 days of the scheduled date. Any updates or modifications to terms must be approved by the Quality Assurance team before implementation. The revised glossary shall be disseminated to all relevant stakeholders and integrated into the organization's training materials. Records of term reviews, modifications, and approvals shall be maintained in a secure electronic repository for a minimum of 3 years.

subpolicies.Description: To implement the Context of the Organization Policy, the organization must establish a structured process for conducting context analysis. This includes identifying internal and external factors, assessing their relevance to OH&S performance, and documenting the findings. Key roles such as the OH&S manager and relevant stakeholders are responsible for conducting this analysis.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S manager shall lead the context analysis process by identifying internal and external factors that can impact OH&S performance. This analysis shall be conducted annually and documented in a structured report. The identified factors and their implications on safety measures must be reviewed by the management team to determine necessary actions for improvement. The context analysis report shall be retained for a minimum of 3 years for audit purposes.

subpolicies.Description: The organization shall conduct a thorough analysis of its internal and external context to identify factors that may impact its OH&S performance. This analysis includes assessing risks, opportunities, legal requirements, and stakeholder expectations. The OH&S management team is responsible for documenting the results of the analysis and ensuring that it is regularly reviewed.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S management team shall review the internal and external context analysis at least annually. The analysis shall be documented, including identified risks, opportunities, and their potential impact on OH&S performance. Top management shall approve any changes resulting from the analysis. The records of the analysis and review shall be retained for a minimum of 3 years for audit purposes.

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Identification and Documentation of Needs and Expectations
subpolicies.Description: To implement this subpolicy, the organization must establish a structured process for identifying and documenting the needs and expectations of workers and other interested parties. This process should involve conducting surveys, interviews, and feedback sessions to gather relevant information. The responsible department should ensure that all identified needs are documented and integrated into the occupational health and safety management system.
subpolicies.Control: The Health and Safety Manager is responsible for overseeing the process of identifying and documenting the needs and expectations. Surveys, interviews, and feedback sessions shall be conducted annually to gather updated information. All documented needs and expectations shall be stored in a centralized database accessible to relevant stakeholders. The success of this control will be measured by the completion of annual surveys and the integration of identified needs into decision-making processes.

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Defining the Scope of the OH&S Management System
subpolicies.Description: To determine the scope of the OH&S management system, the organization shall conduct a thorough assessment of all activities, processes, and locations where workers are exposed to risks. This includes identifying potential hazards, evaluating their significance, and documenting the boundaries of the OH&S management system. The responsible roles for scope determination include [Roles]. The scope statement shall be reviewed annually and updated as necessary.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S management system scope shall be determined by [Responsible Role] through a documented process that includes hazard identification, risk assessment, and stakeholder consultation. The scope statement shall be reviewed annually by [Reviewing Role] to ensure its continued relevance and effectiveness. Any changes to the scope must be approved by [Approving Role] and communicated to all relevant stakeholders. Evidence of scope determination, including hazard assessments and stakeholder feedback, shall be retained for a minimum of [Retention Period].

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment Procedure
subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy outlines the procedure for identifying hazards, assessing risks, and determining controls to mitigate risks within the organization. It includes steps for conducting regular inspections, involving employees in hazard identification, and implementing control measures to eliminate or reduce risks.
subpolicies.Control: The Health & Safety Manager is responsible for conducting regular hazard identification activities at least quarterly. All identified hazards must be documented in the Hazard Register, including the associated risks and proposed control measures. The Hazard Register shall be reviewed by the OH&S Committee monthly, and any overdue actions shall be escalated to the Executive Management. Records of hazard identification, risk assessments, and control measures shall be maintained in a secure electronic database for a minimum of 5 years. The effectiveness of controls shall be monitored through leading and lagging indicators, with a target of reducing high-risk hazards by 20% annually.

subpolicies.Description: To ensure effective leadership engagement, the organization shall conduct regular leadership reviews, set OH&S objectives, and provide necessary resources for implementation. Leadership shall actively participate in hazard identification, risk assessment, and incident investigations. The control aims to establish a clear accountability structure for leadership involvement in OH&S.
subpolicies.Control: Leadership shall conduct quarterly reviews of OH&S performance, set measurable objectives aligned with ISO 45001 requirements, allocate resources for implementation, participate in monthly hazard identification sessions, conduct bi-annual risk assessments, and lead incident investigations when necessary. Records of leadership engagement activities, objectives, and resource allocations shall be maintained for a minimum of 3 years in a secure electronic format accessible to relevant stakeholders.

subpolicies.Description: To promote worker participation, the organization shall establish communication channels, provide training on OH&S matters, and involve workers in decision-making processes. Workers shall have the right to report hazards, suggest improvements, and participate in OH&S committees. The control aims to empower workers to contribute to a safer work environment.
subpolicies.Control: The organization shall conduct monthly worker consultations, provide bi-annual OH&S training sessions, establish a suggestion box for hazard reporting and improvement ideas, and include workers in quarterly OH&S committee meetings. Workers' participation records, training outcomes, and committee meeting minutes shall be documented and retained for a minimum of 5 years in a centralized database accessible to authorized personnel.

subpolicies.Description: To implement this subpolicy, the leadership must actively engage in promoting safety, allocate resources for OH&S initiatives, and participate in risk assessments and incident investigations. Regular communication on safety matters and visible leadership support are essential. Employees should be encouraged to report hazards and provide feedback on safety concerns.
subpolicies.Control: The leadership, including top management, department heads, and supervisors, are responsible for demonstrating visible commitment to occupational health and safety. They must allocate resources for safety initiatives, participate in risk assessments, and investigate incidents. Safety performance reviews should be conducted quarterly, and any nonconformities or incidents identified must be addressed within 48 hours. Records of leadership engagement, resource allocation, and safety performance reviews must be maintained for a minimum of 3 years.

subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy outlines the procedures for reporting and investigating workplace incidents, near-misses, and accidents. It includes steps for immediate response, evidence collection, root cause analysis, and corrective action implementation. Roles such as supervisors, HSE representatives, and affected employees are defined with clear responsibilities. Evidence to collect includes incident reports, witness statements, and corrective action plans. Success is measured by the timeliness of reporting, thoroughness of investigations, and effectiveness of corrective actions. Compliance obligations include ISO 45001 requirements for incident management.
subpolicies.Control: All workplace incidents, near-misses, and accidents must be reported to the designated Health and Safety Executive (HSE) personnel within 24 hours of occurrence. The HSE personnel are responsible for initiating investigations within 48 hours, collecting relevant evidence, conducting root cause analysis, and proposing corrective actions. Incident reports, witness statements, investigation findings, and corrective action plans must be stored securely in a centralized database for a minimum of 5 years. Key performance indicators (KPIs) for incident management effectiveness include the percentage of incidents investigated within the specified timeframe and the recurrence rate of similar incidents. Any exceptions to the reporting or investigation process must be documented, approved by the HSE manager, and escalated if necessary for further review and resolution.

subpolicies.Description: The organization shall clearly define the roles, responsibilities, and authorities of all personnel involved in the OH&S management system. This includes assigning specific tasks for OH&S performance improvement, legal compliance, and achievement of OH&S objectives. Roles and responsibilities shall be documented, communicated, and periodically reviewed for effectiveness.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S manager is responsible for defining and documenting roles, responsibilities, and authorities. This shall be done annually or whenever there are changes in personnel or OH&S requirements. The documentation shall be stored in a centralized repository accessible to all relevant personnel. Regular audits shall be conducted to verify compliance with assigned roles and responsibilities.

subpolicies.Description: To implement worker consultation and participation effectively, the organization must establish clear communication channels, feedback mechanisms, and platforms for workers to provide input on OH&S matters. Roles and responsibilities for facilitating worker engagement should be defined, and mechanisms for documenting consultation outcomes and decisions must be in place. Regular training on consultation processes and OH&S requirements should be provided to ensure effective worker participation.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S Manager is responsible for overseeing the implementation of the worker consultation and participation framework. Workers are required to actively engage in consultation activities, provide feedback, and participate in risk assessments. Consultation outcomes, decisions, and action plans must be documented and stored securely. Training records on consultation processes and OH&S requirements should be maintained. The effectiveness of worker participation shall be monitored through feedback mechanisms and periodic reviews.

subpolicies.Description: Top management must establish, implement, and maintain an OH&S policy that includes commitments to provide safe working conditions, fulfill legal requirements, eliminate hazards, ensure continual improvement, and promote worker consultation and participation. The policy must be documented, communicated, and relevant to the organization's context.
subpolicies.Control: Top management is responsible for ensuring the establishment, implementation, and maintenance of the OH&S policy. The policy must be documented, communicated internally and externally, and regularly reviewed for relevance and effectiveness.

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Assignment of Responsibility for OH&S Conformance
subpolicies.Description: Top management is responsible for ensuring that the OH&S management system conforms to the requirements of ISO 45001. They must also report on the system's performance to top management.
subpolicies.Control: Top management must assign responsibility for ensuring OH&S management system conformance and reporting on performance. This responsibility must be clearly communicated and documented.

subpolicies.Description: The organization must establish, implement, and maintain processes for the consultation and participation of workers at all levels. Workers' consultation should cover various aspects of the OH&S management system.
subpolicies.Control: The organization must provide mechanisms, time, training, and resources for worker consultation and participation. Clear information about the OH&S management system must be provided, and obstacles to participation must be identified and minimized.

subpolicies.Description: The organization shall establish, implement, and maintain an ongoing and proactive hazard identification process that considers various factors such as work organization, social factors, routine and non-routine activities, past incidents, emergency situations, and changes in the organization. The process shall involve regular assessments of infrastructure, equipment, materials, human factors, work performance, and other relevant issues to ensure a comprehensive understanding of workplace hazards.
subpolicies.Control: The Hazard Identification Process shall be conducted by designated safety officers on a quarterly basis. The process shall involve reviewing work organization, social factors, routine and non-routine activities, past incidents, emergency situations, and changes in the organization. The findings and identified hazards shall be documented, stored electronically in a secure database, and retained for a minimum of 5 years. Success metrics include the number of hazards identified, the timeliness of assessments, and the effectiveness of hazard mitigation measures. Any exceptions or deviations from the process must be documented, reviewed by the safety committee, and approved by the Safety Manager.

subpolicies.Description: Top management must establish, implement, and maintain an OH&S policy that provides safe working conditions, fulfills legal requirements, and promotes consultation and participation of workers. The policy should be communicated within the organization, available to interested parties, and relevant to the organization's purpose, size, and context.
subpolicies.Control: Top management is responsible for ensuring that the OH&S policy is documented, communicated, and available to interested parties. The policy must include commitments to provide safe working conditions, fulfill legal requirements, eliminate hazards, reduce OH&S risks, and promote continual improvement. The effectiveness of the policy should be periodically reviewed and updated as necessary.

subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy provides detailed guidance on conducting risk assessments, implementing controls, and monitoring effectiveness to manage occupational health and safety risks. It outlines the stepwise process for identifying hazards, assessing risks, prioritizing control measures, and monitoring their effectiveness. Key roles include the HSE manager, supervisors, and employees involved in risk assessment activities.
subpolicies.Control: The HSE manager is responsible for conducting regular risk assessments at defined intervals or when significant changes occur. Employees must report hazards promptly, and supervisors are accountable for implementing control measures within specified timelines. All risk assessment findings, control measures, and effectiveness evaluations must be documented and retained for a minimum of [X] years in a centralized database accessible to relevant stakeholders. Success metrics include the percentage of identified risks with implemented controls and the reduction in incident rates due to effective risk management.

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment Process
subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy outlines the step-by-step process for conducting hazard identification and risk assessment activities. It includes tools, methodologies, and roles involved in the process. The subpolicy provides detailed guidance on how to document, prioritize, and mitigate identified risks.
subpolicies.Control: The hazard identification and risk assessment process shall be conducted annually by a designated team comprising representatives from different organizational levels. The process shall include the identification of hazards, evaluation of risks using a risk matrix, prioritization of risks based on severity and likelihood, and development of risk mitigation strategies. The results of the hazard identification and risk assessment shall be documented, communicated to relevant stakeholders, and reviewed periodically for effectiveness.

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Legal and Other Requirements Determination Process
subpolicies.Description: To implement the Legal and Other Requirements Determination Policy, the organization must establish a structured process for identifying, monitoring, and evaluating legal and other requirements related to occupational health and safety. This process should involve regular assessments, documentation of obligations, and proactive measures to ensure compliance.
subpolicies.Control: The Legal and Other Requirements Determination Process shall be overseen by the OH&S management team, with responsibility assigned to the Compliance Officer. Legal requirements and other obligations shall be reviewed quarterly, with updates documented in a centralized repository. Non-compliance instances shall trigger immediate corrective actions, and evidence of compliance shall be retained for a minimum of 5 years in a secure electronic format.

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Actions to Address Risks and Opportunities
subpolicies.Description: To address risks and opportunities, the organization shall consider contextual issues, interested parties, and the OH&S management system scope. It must identify hazards, assess risks, and determine control measures to achieve intended outcomes, prevent undesired effects, and drive continual improvement. Legal and other requirements must be integrated into the planning process to ensure compliance and effective risk management.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S management team is responsible for conducting risk assessments, identifying hazards, and assessing risks and opportunities. This process must be conducted annually, or whenever significant changes occur in the organization, processes, or the OH&S management system. Risk assessments shall be documented, stored securely, and reviewed periodically to ensure effectiveness. Key performance indicators (KPIs) related to risk mitigation and opportunity realization shall be monitored regularly to track progress and compliance with ISO 45001 standards.

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment Process
subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy provides detailed guidance on establishing, implementing, and maintaining a hazard identification and risk assessment process in compliance with ISO 45001:2018. It includes step-by-step instructions, roles and responsibilities, tools to be used, evidence to collect, and success criteria for effective risk management.
subpolicies.Control: The organization shall establish, implement, and maintain an ongoing and proactive process for hazard identification, considering various factors such as work organization, social aspects, routine and non-routine activities, past incidents, emergency situations, and changes in operations. The responsible roles include OH&S managers, supervisors, and designated safety personnel. This process shall be conducted regularly, at least annually, and documented records of identified hazards, assessed risks, and planned actions shall be retained for a minimum of [X] years in a secure electronic database. Key performance indicators (KPIs) for successful hazard identification and risk assessment include the percentage of identified hazards addressed within specified timelines and the effectiveness of risk mitigation measures. Any exceptions or deviations from the process must be documented, reviewed, and approved by the OH&S management team.

subpolicies.Description: To implement the hazard identification process effectively, the organization must establish, implement, and maintain a systematic approach that considers work organization, social factors, routine and non-routine activities, past incidents, emergency situations, and organizational changes. Key steps include conducting regular hazard assessments, involving relevant stakeholders, documenting identified hazards, and updating the hazard register accordingly.
subpolicies.Control: The Hazard Identification Process shall be conducted ongoing and proactively, considering work organization, social factors, routine and non-routine activities, past incidents, emergency situations, and organizational changes. The process must involve relevant stakeholders, document identified hazards, update the hazard register, and ensure timely communication of identified risks to the appropriate personnel.

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment Process
subpolicies.Description: To effectively implement the hazard identification and risk assessment process, the organization must establish, implement, and maintain an ongoing and proactive approach. This process should consider various factors such as work organization, routine and non-routine activities, past incidents, emergency situations, people involved, changes in operations, and knowledge of hazards.
subpolicies.Control: The responsible department shall conduct ongoing hazard identification activities, considering factors like work organization, routine and non-routine activities, past incidents, emergency situations, and changes in operations. The process must involve relevant stakeholders, document identified hazards, assess associated risks, and implement controls to mitigate risks. Hazard identification and risk assessment records shall be retained for a minimum of [X] years in a secure electronic repository accessible to authorized personnel. The success of this control shall be monitored through regular audits, inspections, and incident reviews, with any deviations or non-conformities reported and escalated to the OH&S management team for corrective action.

subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy provides detailed guidance on conducting risk and opportunity assessments, integrating legal requirements, and planning actions to address identified risks and opportunities.
subpolicies.Control: The responsible personnel shall conduct regular assessments of OH&S risks, opportunities, legal requirements, and other related risks using defined methodologies and criteria. The assessments must be proactive, systematic, and documented. Legal requirements and other applicable requirements shall be integrated into the OH&S management system processes. Actions to address identified risks, opportunities, and legal requirements must be planned, implemented, and evaluated for effectiveness. The hierarchy of controls and outputs from the OH&S management system shall be considered in the planning process.

subpolicies.Description: Establish a systematic process to assess OH&S risks, considering existing controls. Define methodologies and criteria for risk assessments to ensure proactive identification of hazards. Maintain documented information on methodologies and criteria.
subpolicies.Control: The EHS Manager is responsible for conducting OH&S risk assessments annually. The methodology must include hazard identification, effectiveness of controls, and proactive risk mitigation strategies. Documented records of assessments and criteria must be stored in a secure electronic repository for a minimum of 5 years. Success is measured by the completion of assessments and the implementation of risk mitigation actions.

subpolicies.Description: Establish a process to determine and access up-to-date legal requirements applicable to hazards, OH&S risks, and the management system. Ensure legal requirements are considered in the establishment and improvement of the OH&S management system.
subpolicies.Control: The Legal Compliance Officer is responsible for monitoring and updating legal requirements quarterly. Legal requirements must be communicated to relevant stakeholders promptly. Documented information on legal requirements and their applicability to the organization must be stored in a centralized legal repository. Compliance with legal requirements is monitored through regular audits and reported to the Compliance Committee.

subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy provides detailed guidance on conducting competence assessments for occupational health and safety risks, opportunities, legal requirements, and other related risks and opportunities. It includes defining assessment methodologies, criteria, and responsibilities, documenting assessment results, and planning actions to address identified areas.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S competence assessments shall be conducted by designated competent personnel at regular intervals as defined by the organization's assessment schedule. The assessment methodologies and criteria shall be documented and reviewed periodically to ensure relevance and effectiveness. The responsible roles for conducting assessments, documenting results, and planning actions shall be clearly defined. Assessment records shall be maintained in a secure and accessible manner for a minimum period of [X] years. The success of the assessments shall be measured based on the timely identification of risks, opportunities, legal requirements, and other related areas, and the effectiveness of actions taken to address them. Any exceptions or deviations from the assessment process shall be documented, reviewed, and approved by the designated authority.

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Assessment of OH&S Risks and Other Risks
subpolicies.Description: To implement the assessment of OH&S risks and other risks, the organization must define methodologies and criteria for proactive risk assessment. Responsible roles include OH&S managers and risk assessment teams. The assessment process should be conducted regularly, considering identified hazards and existing controls. Documented information on methodologies, criteria, and assessment results must be retained for review and improvement.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S managers and risk assessment teams are responsible for assessing OH&S risks and other risks regularly, considering identified hazards and existing controls. The assessment process should be proactive, systematic, and documented. Results of the assessment, methodologies, and criteria must be retained for a specified period in a secure location accessible for review and audit purposes.

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Assessment of OH&S Opportunities and Other Opportunities
subpolicies.Description: To assess OH&S opportunities and other opportunities, the organization must identify areas for enhancing OH&S performance and improving the OH&S management system. Responsible roles include OH&S improvement teams and management representatives. Opportunities to adapt work, eliminate hazards, and improve the OH&S management system should be systematically evaluated and documented.
subpolicies.Control: OH&S improvement teams and management representatives are responsible for identifying and assessing OH&S opportunities and other opportunities systematically. Opportunities to enhance performance, adapt work, and improve the management system should be documented and retained for future reference. The assessment process should be proactive, considering planned changes and organizational activities.

subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy outlines the methodology for assessing OH&S risks, including hazard identification, risk evaluation, and control effectiveness assessment. The process involves proactive risk assessment, systematic use of methodologies and criteria, and maintenance of documented information. Key roles include OH&S managers, risk assessors, and document controllers.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S risk assessment methodology shall be implemented by OH&S managers and risk assessors. It should be conducted regularly, at least annually, using standardized risk assessment tools and templates. The responsible parties shall document the assessment results, including identified hazards, risk levels, and control effectiveness. All assessment records shall be stored securely for a minimum of 5 years. Success metrics include the completion of timely risk assessments, effective control implementation, and continuous improvement in OH&S performance. Any exceptions or deviations from the assessment process must be approved by the OH&S manager and documented for review.

subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy details the process for determining legal requirements applicable to hazards, risks, and the OH&S management system. It includes accessing up-to-date legal requirements, assessing their applicability, and communicating relevant requirements to stakeholders. Legal compliance teams, OH&S managers, and document controllers play crucial roles in this process.
subpolicies.Control: Legal requirements shall be determined by legal compliance teams and OH&S managers. Access to up-to-date legal databases and regulatory sources shall be maintained. Legal requirements shall be reviewed quarterly and communicated to relevant stakeholders. The applicability of legal requirements to the organization's OH&S management system shall be documented, including actions needed for compliance. All legal requirement documentation shall be retained for a minimum of 7 years. Key success metrics include timely updates on legal requirements, accurate determination of applicability, and proactive compliance measures. Any deviations or non-compliance issues shall be escalated to the legal compliance team for resolution.

subpolicies.Description: The organization shall establish a clear internal communication process to prepare for and respond to emergency situations. This process should include defining roles and responsibilities, identifying required resources, setting timelines for completion, evaluating results, and integrating actions into business processes. Communication channels, methods, and frequency should be determined to ensure effective dissemination of information.
subpolicies.Control: The internal communication process shall be the responsibility of the OH&S management team, with designated individuals responsible for specific communication tasks. Communication activities should be conducted in real-time during emergencies and documented for future evaluation. Communication effectiveness shall be measured through response times, clarity of information, and feedback mechanisms. Records of communication activities and outcomes shall be retained for a minimum period of [X] years in a secure and accessible format.

subpolicies.Description: To implement the External Communication Policy, the organization must establish a structured process for external communication related to OH&S. This process should include preparing for and responding to emergency situations, integrating actions into the OH&S management system processes, evaluating effectiveness, and considering best practices, technological options, and financial requirements. Roles and responsibilities for external communication must be clearly defined, resources allocated, timelines set, and evaluation indicators established.
subpolicies.Control: The External Communication Process shall be overseen by the OH&S manager, who is responsible for ensuring timely and effective communication during emergency situations. Communication tools and channels shall be identified and maintained, with regular drills and exercises to test the communication process. Records of external communication activities, including responses to emergencies, shall be retained for a minimum of [X] years in a secure and accessible format. The success of the External Communication Process shall be measured through the timeliness and accuracy of communication during emergencies, with any deviations or failures reported and addressed promptly.

subpolicies.Description: To implement this subpolicy, designated personnel must document all OH&S objectives in a clear and measurable manner. The objectives should be specific, achievable, relevant, and time-bound. Tools such as templates or software may be used to facilitate the documentation process. The documented objectives must be communicated to all relevant stakeholders for awareness and alignment.
subpolicies.Control: Designated OH&S managers are responsible for documenting OH&S objectives annually, ensuring that they are SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound). The documentation process should be completed by the end of the first quarter each year using standardized templates stored in the centralized OH&S management system. The success of this control will be monitored through quarterly reviews and audits to verify the completeness and alignment of documented OH&S objectives.

subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy requires the development of detailed plans to achieve the documented OH&S objectives. The plans should outline specific actions, responsibilities, timelines, and resources required for successful implementation. Regular updates and reviews of the plans are essential to adapt to changing circumstances and ensure continuous improvement.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S planning team, led by the OH&S director, is responsible for developing and updating the OH&S plans in alignment with the documented objectives. The plans must be finalized within one month of documenting the objectives and should include clear action steps, assigned responsibilities, timelines, and resource allocations. The OH&S director will review and approve the plans quarterly to ensure relevance and effectiveness. Any deviations or delays in plan implementation must be documented and escalated for timely resolution.

subpolicies.Description: The organization shall conduct regular competence assessments to identify training needs. Competence gaps shall be addressed through appropriate training programs. Competence evaluations shall be documented and reviewed periodically to ensure effectiveness.
subpolicies.Control: The HR department is responsible for conducting competence assessments annually. Training programs shall be developed based on assessment outcomes. Competence evaluation records shall be stored in the HR database for a minimum of 3 years. The success metric is a 90% compliance rate with the training program completion.

subpolicies.Description: To implement the control of documented information, the organization must establish a documented procedure outlining the process for controlling all forms of documented information within the OH&S management system. This includes defining roles and responsibilities, specifying document formats, establishing review and approval processes, determining access controls, and setting retention periods.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S Manager is responsible for developing the documented procedure for controlling documented information. The procedure must be established within 30 days of policy approval and reviewed annually. The procedure shall outline the format requirements, approval process involving the OH&S Committee, access controls limiting document modification to authorized personnel, and retention period of at least 5 years. The procedure effectiveness shall be monitored through annual audits and compliance assessments.

subpolicies.Description: To ensure competence, the organization shall evaluate workers' skills affecting OH&S performance and provide necessary training. Competence shall be assessed based on education, training, or experience. The control includes regular competence assessments, training programs, and documentation of competence levels.
subpolicies.Control: The HR department is responsible for evaluating workers' competence annually. Training programs shall be conducted bi-annually for all workers. Competence assessments and training records shall be stored electronically for a minimum of 5 years. The success metric is a 90% compliance rate with training requirements. Any exceptions to the training schedule must be approved by the HR manager.

subpolicies.Description: The organization shall establish internal and external communication processes relevant to the OH&S management system. This includes determining what, when, and how to communicate, considering diversity aspects. The control involves regular communication audits, feedback mechanisms, and documentation of communication processes.
subpolicies.Control: The Communication Department is responsible for establishing and maintaining internal and external communication processes. Communication audits shall be conducted quarterly to ensure effectiveness. Feedback mechanisms such as suggestion boxes and surveys shall be utilized. All communication records shall be stored securely for a minimum of 3 years. The success metric is a 95% satisfaction rate with internal communication processes. Any deviations from the communication plan must be documented and approved by the Communication Manager.

subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy outlines the requirements for evaluating workers' competence, providing necessary training, and maintaining documented evidence of competence. It includes identifying necessary competencies, providing appropriate education and training, and evaluating the effectiveness of competence actions.
subpolicies.Control: The HR department is responsible for determining necessary worker competencies and providing relevant training. Competence evaluations should be conducted annually, with documented evidence stored in the HR training records. Training effectiveness should be assessed through post-training evaluations, and any gaps in competence addressed through additional training or re-assignment.

subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy details the internal and external communication processes required for effective operational planning and control. It includes establishing communication channels, considering diversity aspects, responding to relevant communications, and retaining documented information.
subpolicies.Control: The Communication Manager is responsible for establishing and maintaining internal and external communication processes. Internal communication should include updates on OH&S management system changes, while external communication should align with legal requirements. Documented information on communications should be stored in a centralized repository accessible to relevant stakeholders.

subpolicies.Description: To implement the internal communication process, the organization must ensure that relevant OH&S information is shared among different levels and functions. Workers should be enabled to contribute to continual improvement through effective communication channels. The process should be documented and accessible to all relevant personnel.
subpolicies.Control: The internal communication process shall be overseen by the OH&S management team, with regular communication updates scheduled monthly. The responsible roles include OH&S managers and supervisors. Documentation of internal communications shall be stored electronically in a centralized repository for a minimum of 3 years, with periodic audits to ensure compliance.

subpolicies.Description: The external communication process involves sharing OH&S information with external parties as per legal requirements. The organization must establish clear channels for external communication and ensure that the information shared is accurate, reliable, and compliant with regulations.
subpolicies.Control: External communication shall be managed by the OH&S communication team, with updates communicated quarterly to external stakeholders. The responsible roles include OH&S communication officers and legal compliance officers. Records of external communications shall be maintained in both physical and electronic formats for a minimum of 5 years, with regular reviews for accuracy and compliance.

subpolicies.Description: To implement the Management of Change Policy, the organization must establish clear communication processes that consider diversity aspects and legal requirements. This includes internal communication among different levels and functions, as well as external communication related to the OH&S management system.
subpolicies.Control: The responsible department shall define and document communication processes in alignment with diversity aspects and legal requirements. Internal communication shall be conducted regularly, ensuring changes to the OH&S management system are effectively communicated. External communication shall adhere to established processes and legal obligations. Documented information on communications shall be retained for evidence.

subpolicies.Description: To establish effective communication processes, the organization must consider diversity aspects, legal requirements, and the reliability of OH&S information. Internal communication should facilitate continual improvement, while external communication must align with legal obligations. Documented information retention is essential to evidence communication activities.
subpolicies.Control: The organization's communication processes must be established to consider diversity aspects, legal requirements, and OH&S information reliability. Internal communication should enable continual improvement, and external communication must comply with legal obligations. Documented information retention is mandatory to evidence communication activities. Responsible roles include OH&S managers, communication officers, and relevant department heads. Communication processes should be reviewed annually, with documented evidence stored in a secure digital repository for a minimum of 5 years. Success metrics include the timeliness of responses to communications, stakeholder engagement levels, and the accuracy of documented information.

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Documented Information Management for Emergency Preparedness
subpolicies.Description: To effectively manage documented information for emergency preparedness, the organization must establish clear identification, appropriate formats, and regular review processes. Documented information shall be controlled to ensure availability, protection, and integrity. Activities such as distribution, storage, version control, and retention must be managed effectively to support emergency response.
subpolicies.Control: The responsible personnel (e.g., OH&S manager) shall review and update documented information for emergency preparedness regularly (at least annually). The information shall be stored electronically and in hard copy, with access restricted to authorized personnel. Changes to the information must be documented, version-controlled, and retained for a minimum of [X] years. Regular audits shall be conducted to ensure compliance and effectiveness.

subpolicies.Description: The organization shall establish clear criteria for performance evaluations of the occupational health and safety management system. Criteria should include measurable indicators for assessing the effectiveness of OH&S processes, controls, and actions determined in Clause 6 of ISO 45001:2018. The criteria should be reviewed and updated regularly to ensure relevance and alignment with organizational goals and regulatory requirements.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S manager is responsible for defining and updating the performance evaluation criteria annually. The criteria shall be reviewed by the OH&S committee for suitability and adequacy. Performance evaluations shall be conducted bi-annually, with documented evidence of the evaluation results, findings, and proposed actions. The results of the evaluations shall be communicated to relevant stakeholders, including management and workers. The documented information related to performance evaluations shall be retained for a minimum of 3 years in electronic format in a secure, accessible location.

subpolicies.Description: To control documented information, the organization shall ensure appropriate identification, format, review, and approval. Documented information shall be available, suitable for use, adequately protected, and controlled for distribution, access, storage, preservation, changes, retention, and disposition. External documented information necessary for the OH&S management system shall also be identified and controlled.
subpolicies.Control: Documented information control shall be overseen by the Document Control Manager, who will ensure that all documented information is appropriately identified, formatted, reviewed, and approved. The control activities include managing distribution, access, storage, preservation, changes, retention, and disposition of documented information. The success of this control will be measured by the accuracy and timeliness of documented information updates, the protection of information integrity, and compliance with version control procedures.

subpolicies.Description: The organization shall establish a structured compliance evaluation process to assess adherence to OH&S controls and requirements. This process includes regular audits, inspections, and reviews to identify gaps and non-conformities. The responsible department shall conduct thorough evaluations, document findings, and implement corrective actions to address identified issues.
subpolicies.Control: The Compliance department is responsible for conducting quarterly compliance evaluations to assess adherence to OH&S controls. Audits shall be conducted using standardized checklists and procedures. Findings and corrective actions shall be documented in the compliance evaluation report, which must be retained for a minimum of 3 years. The success of the compliance evaluation process shall be measured by the percentage of identified non-conformities addressed within the specified timeframe. Any exceptions or unresolved issues shall be escalated to the OH&S management team for further action.

subpolicies.Description: The organization shall establish a structured process for managing changes that impact OH&S performance, including new products, services, processes, legal requirements, and knowledge updates. This process involves assessing the impact of changes on OH&S risks, implementing mitigation measures, and reviewing the consequences of unintended changes.
subpolicies.Control: The Change Management department is responsible for reviewing all proposed changes that impact OH&S performance. A Change Impact Assessment must be conducted to evaluate potential risks and necessary mitigation measures. Changes shall be approved by the OH&S management team before implementation. Records of change assessments, approvals, and mitigation actions shall be maintained for a minimum of 5 years. The success of the change management process shall be measured by the percentage of changes implemented without adverse OH&S effects.

subpolicies.Description: The organization shall establish a process to control the procurement of products and services to ensure conformity to its OH&S management system. This process includes assessing suppliers, verifying product/service conformity, and monitoring procurement activities to prevent OH&S risks.
subpolicies.Control: The Procurement department is responsible for assessing suppliers based on OH&S criteria, verifying product/service conformity, and monitoring procurement activities. Supplier assessments shall be conducted annually, and non-conforming products/services shall be rejected. Records of supplier assessments, product verifications, and procurement monitoring shall be retained for a minimum of 3 years. The success of the procurement control process shall be measured by the percentage of compliant products/services procured within the organization.

subpolicies.Description: The internal audit process shall be conducted by trained auditors who are independent of the audited areas. Auditors shall follow a documented audit plan, conduct on-site inspections, interview personnel, and review relevant documentation. The audit findings shall be recorded, analyzed, and reported to management for review and action.
subpolicies.Control: The internal audit shall be conducted annually, covering all OH&S management system elements. The audit plan, findings, and corrective actions shall be documented and retained for a minimum of 3 years. The audit reports shall include identified hazards, assessed risks, control effectiveness, and recommendations for improvement. The OH&S manager is responsible for overseeing the internal audit process, ensuring timely corrective actions, and monitoring the implementation of audit recommendations.

subpolicies.Description: To implement this policy, the organization must establish a documented internal audit program specifying the frequency, scope, and methodologies for conducting internal audits. Designated auditors should conduct audits, document findings, and report results to management. Non-conformities identified during audits must be addressed through corrective actions within defined timelines. The effectiveness of corrective actions should be verified through follow-up audits.
subpolicies.Control: The organization's OH&S management representative is responsible for developing and maintaining the internal audit program. Internal audits shall be conducted annually by trained auditors using standardized audit checklists. Audit findings and corrective actions shall be documented in the audit reports stored in a secure electronic repository for a minimum of 5 years. The success of this control shall be monitored through the timely closure of non-conformities and the effectiveness of corrective actions verified through subsequent audits.

subpolicies.Description: To implement the Internal Audit Programme, the organization must establish audit schedules, define audit criteria, select competent auditors, conduct audits, document findings, communicate results, implement corrective actions, and monitor effectiveness. The programme must include a systematic approach to planning, conducting, and reporting audits, ensuring compliance with OH&S requirements and continuous improvement.
subpolicies.Control: The Internal Audit Programme implementation shall be overseen by the OH&S Manager, who is responsible for scheduling audits, defining criteria, selecting auditors, conducting audits, documenting findings, communicating results, implementing corrective actions, and monitoring effectiveness. Audits shall be conducted annually, with documented records retained for a minimum of 3 years. The audit process shall include pre-audit meetings, on-site inspections, interviews, and post-audit reporting. Non-conformities identified during audits must be addressed within specified timelines, with effectiveness verified through follow-up audits.

subpolicies.Description: To manage contractors' activities impacting interested parties, the organization must define and apply occupational health and safety criteria for contractor selection. The control includes specifying the criteria in procurement processes and contractual documents, ensuring compliance with OH&S requirements, and maintaining records of contractor evaluations.
subpolicies.Control: The organization's procurement processes must define and apply occupational health and safety criteria for contractor selection. These criteria should be included in contractual documents. Compliance with OH&S requirements must be ensured, and records of contractor evaluations must be maintained for review.

subpolicies.Description: To control outsourced functions and processes, the organization must ensure alignment with legal and system requirements. The control involves defining the type and degree of control within the OH&S management system, coordinating with external providers, and maintaining documented information on outsourcing arrangements.
subpolicies.Control: Outsourced functions and processes must be controlled to align with legal and system requirements. The type and degree of control should be defined within the OH&S management system. Coordination with external providers is essential, and documented information on outsourcing arrangements must be maintained.

subpolicies.Description: To establish effective emergency preparedness and response processes, the organization must plan, train, test, evaluate, and communicate responses to potential emergency situations. The control includes providing first aid, training workers, testing response capabilities, and communicating with all stakeholders.
subpolicies.Control: The organization must establish planned responses to emergency situations, provide first aid, train workers, test response capabilities, and communicate with all stakeholders. Testing and evaluation of response plans must be conducted periodically, and relevant information should be communicated to all parties.

subpolicies.Description: The organization shall plan, establish, and maintain an internal audit program to assess the conformity and effectiveness of the OH&S management system. This includes defining audit criteria, selecting auditors, reporting audit results, and taking corrective actions. The audit program must consider the importance of processes, previous audit results, and involve relevant stakeholders.
subpolicies.Control: Internal audits shall be conducted at planned intervals to evaluate the organization's compliance with OH&S requirements. The audit program must define audit criteria, select impartial auditors, report results to management and workers, and address nonconformities. Documented information on audit implementation and results shall be retained for review.

subpolicies.Description: Top management shall conduct periodic reviews of the OH&S management system to ensure its suitability, adequacy, and effectiveness. The review process includes assessing actions from previous reviews, changes in internal and external issues, OH&S policy compliance, performance trends, resource adequacy, and opportunities for improvement.
subpolicies.Control: Management reviews shall be conducted at planned intervals to evaluate the OH&S management system's continuing suitability and effectiveness. Reviews must consider changes in internal and external issues, compliance with legal requirements, OH&S performance trends, resource adequacy, and opportunities for improvement. Results of management reviews and identified improvement opportunities shall be documented and communicated to relevant stakeholders.

subpolicies.Description: To implement the Internal Audit Conduct Policy, the organization must establish a structured internal audit program. This program should define the frequency, scope, and methodology of internal audits. Appoint qualified internal auditors responsible for conducting audits. Develop audit criteria based on organizational requirements, OH&S policy, objectives, and ISO 45001 standard. Ensure that audit results are documented, communicated, and acted upon promptly.
subpolicies.Control: Internal audits shall be conducted by qualified internal auditors at planned intervals as defined in the internal audit program. The audits must assess conformity with organizational requirements, OH&S policy, objectives, and the ISO 45001 standard. Audit results, including non-conformities and corrective actions, shall be documented and communicated to relevant stakeholders. The responsible department shall ensure timely corrective actions are taken to address identified non-conformities and continuously improve the OH&S management system.

subpolicies.Description: This procedure outlines the steps for reporting incidents, initiating investigations, determining root causes, and implementing corrective actions. It involves timely reporting, thorough investigation, and documentation of findings to prevent recurrence.
subpolicies.Control: All incidents must be reported immediately by the involved personnel to the designated OH&S officer. Investigations shall be initiated within 24 hours of the incident, involving relevant stakeholders. Root cause analysis must be conducted within 5 days, and corrective actions implemented within 10 days. All findings, actions taken, and outcomes shall be documented and retained for a minimum of 2 years.

subpolicies.Description: To implement effective monitoring and measurement processes, the organization must establish clear performance indicators, define measurement methodologies, assign responsibilities for data collection and analysis, set frequency for monitoring activities, and document results. Regular reviews of monitoring data should be conducted to identify trends, areas for improvement, and compliance gaps.
subpolicies.Control: The Health and Safety Manager is responsible for defining key performance indicators, the Safety Officer is responsible for data collection, the HR Department is responsible for worker participation, monitoring activities should be conducted monthly, monitoring results should be documented in the OH&S management system, and any deviations from expected performance levels should trigger immediate corrective actions.

subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy outlines the process for reporting, investigating, and taking action on incidents and nonconformities within the OH&S management system. It includes steps for reacting to incidents, evaluating root causes, implementing corrective actions, and assessing risks. The involvement of workers and relevant interested parties is crucial in this process.
subpolicies.Control: When an incident or nonconformity occurs, the organization shall react promptly, control and correct the issue, evaluate root causes with worker participation, review existing risk assessments, implement corrective actions following the hierarchy of controls, assess new hazards, and review the effectiveness of actions taken. Documented information on incidents, nonconformities, actions, and corrective actions shall be retained and communicated to relevant parties.

subpolicies.SubPolicyName: Encouraging Incident Reporting and Worker Participation
subpolicies.Description: To implement this subpolicy, the organization must establish clear procedures for incident reporting and encourage worker participation in OH&S initiatives. Workers should feel empowered to report hazards, risks, and opportunities without fear of reprisals.
subpolicies.Control: The organization must designate responsible OH&S personnel to receive incident reports, investigate them promptly, and take corrective actions. Workers should be informed about the incident reporting process, including how to report, who to report to, and the protection against reprisals. Incident reports must be documented, analyzed, and used to improve the OH&S management system. Regular reviews of incident reports should be conducted to identify trends and areas for improvement.

subpolicies.Description: The organization shall establish a structured process for worker consultation and participation in occupational health and safety matters. This process includes regular dialogue, feedback mechanisms, and opportunities for workers to contribute to decision-making processes. Workers' input shall be considered in the planning and implementation of OH&S measures.
subpolicies.Control: The Worker Consultation and Participation Process shall involve regular meetings between workers and management to discuss OH&S issues. Workers shall have the opportunity to provide feedback on proposed changes and hazard identification. The responsible department shall document all feedback received and actions taken in response. Meetings shall be held at least quarterly, with additional ad-hoc meetings scheduled as needed. Meeting minutes and action plans shall be retained for a minimum of two years for audit purposes.

subpolicies.Description: To assess OH&S opportunities, the organization shall identify potential improvements in OH&S performance, evaluate their benefits, and determine their impact on the OH&S management system. This assessment should be conducted regularly and involve relevant stakeholders. The control process should include defining WHO is responsible for the assessment, WHAT methods are used, WHEN assessments occur, HOW benefits are evaluated, and WHERE assessment records are stored.
subpolicies.Control: The OH&S opportunities assessment shall be conducted by the OH&S management team quarterly. The EHS Manager is responsible for leading the assessment using a combination of surveys, interviews, and observations. Assessment results and improvement recommendations shall be documented in the OH&S opportunities register stored in the centralized EHS database. Success metrics include a 10% improvement in OH&S performance within six months of implementing identified opportunities.

subpolicies.Description: To implement the hierarchy of controls, the organization must follow specific steps for hazard elimination, substitution, engineering controls, administrative controls, and personal protective equipment provision. This includes defining work organization, inducting new workers, redefining processes, and providing ergonomic approaches. The control measures must be implemented by the Health and Safety Department.
subpolicies.Control: The Health and Safety Department is responsible for implementing the hierarchy of controls. Hazard elimination should be prioritized by removing hazards, substituting hazardous materials, implementing engineering controls, conducting safety training, and providing personal protective equipment. Controls must be applied continuously, with regular inspections and training sessions. Records of hazard assessments, control measures, and training sessions must be maintained for a minimum of 3 years.

subpolicies.Description: The management of change process aims to minimize the introduction of new hazards and risks into the work environment. It involves assessing occupational health and safety risks associated with changes in technology, equipment, facilities, and work practices. The organization must use appropriate methodologies for risk assessment and ensure effective communication during change processes.
subpolicies.Control: The Health and Safety Department must conduct risk assessments for all changes in technology, equipment, facilities, and work practices. The organization should use design reviews and other methodologies to assess occupational health and safety risks. Changes must be communicated to all relevant stakeholders, and records of risk assessments and change management processes must be retained for audit purposes.

subpolicies.Description: The procurement process should assess and eliminate hazards in products, materials, equipment, and services before their introduction into the workplace. Contractor coordination is essential to ensure external providers adhere to occupational health and safety requirements. The organization must verify contractors' capabilities, performance records, and compliance with health and safety standards.
subpolicies.Control: The Procurement Department is responsible for assessing and eliminating hazards in products, materials, equipment, and services. Contractors must meet health and safety performance criteria, provide safety training, and comply with the organization's occupational health and safety standards. The organization should maintain records of contractor assessments, qualifications, and safety performance for a minimum of 5 years.

subpolicies.Description: When outsourcing functions, the organization must retain control over the outsourced processes to ensure compliance with occupational health and safety requirements. Factors such as external organization capabilities, technical competence, and impact on the organization's OH&S management system must be considered. Legal requirements related to outsourced functions should also be addressed.
subpolicies.Control: The organization's Procurement and Legal Departments are responsible for assessing and retaining control over outsourced functions. Factors such as external organization capabilities, technical competence, and impact on the organization's OH&S management system must be evaluated. Legal requirements related to outsourced functions should be documented and monitored regularly.

subpolicies.Description: To implement this subpolicy, the organization must establish criteria for comparison and use indicators for measurement. Criteria can include incidents, legal requirements, and other relevant factors. Indicators should be selected based on the established criteria to measure performance effectively.
subpolicies.Control: The responsible OH&S manager shall establish criteria for comparison, select appropriate indicators for measurement, and ensure that indicators are consistently used. Criteria and indicators shall be reviewed periodically to ensure relevance and effectiveness. Records of criteria, indicator selection, and measurement results shall be maintained in a centralized database for a minimum of 3 years.

subpolicies.Description: To ensure objectivity and impartiality in internal audits, the organization shall implement a process that separates auditors' roles from their normal duties. Internal auditors should not audit their own work. External auditors should be engaged for independent assessments. The audit process should be documented, including auditor selection criteria and audit frequency.
subpolicies.Control: Internal auditors shall not audit their own work. External auditors engaged for internal audits must be independent and free from conflicts of interest. The organization shall document the internal audit process, including criteria for auditor selection and audit frequency. The results of internal audits shall be reviewed by management to verify compliance and identify areas for improvement.

subpolicies.Description: To effectively manage incidents, nonconformities, and corrective actions, the organization shall establish documented processes for incident reporting, investigation, and corrective action implementation. Root cause analysis shall be conducted to identify underlying factors contributing to incidents and nonconformities. Corrective actions shall be based on the hierarchy of controls to prevent reoccurrence.
subpolicies.Control: Incidents, nonconformities, and corrective actions shall be promptly reported, documented, and investigated. Root cause analysis shall be conducted to identify contributing factors. Corrective actions shall be implemented based on the hierarchy of controls, including hazard elimination, substitution, engineering controls, administrative controls, and personal protective equipment. The effectiveness of corrective actions shall be monitored to prevent reoccurrence.

subpolicies.Description: This subpolicy provides detailed guidance on implementing the continual improvement process within the organization's occupational health and safety management system. It outlines the steps to identify improvement opportunities, assess their impact, implement changes, and monitor the effectiveness of improvement initiatives. Roles such as top management, OH&S personnel, and employees are responsible for driving the continual improvement process. Evidence collection includes records of improvement actions, performance metrics, and feedback from stakeholders. Success is measured by the reduction of risks, enhanced performance, and increased employee engagement.
subpolicies.Control: Top management is responsible for initiating the continual improvement process by identifying improvement opportunities based on new technologies, best practices, stakeholder feedback, and resource optimization. OH&S personnel shall assess the impact of proposed improvements on safety and well-being, implement changes in procedures or processes, and monitor the effectiveness of the implemented improvements. Records of improvement actions, performance metrics, and stakeholder feedback shall be maintained in a centralized repository for a minimum of [X] years. Key success metrics include the reduction of workplace risks, enhanced OH&S performance, and increased employee engagement. Any exceptions to the improvement process must be documented, reviewed, and approved by top management to ensure alignment with the organization's objectives and regulatory requirements.
